1: hōdû lYHWH kî-ṭôb kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "give thanks to the LORD, for he is good — for his steadfast love endures forever" — the refrain kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô (for his steadfast love endures forever) occurs 26 times — once per verse — making this the only biblical psalm with an unbroken antiphonal refrain throughout. ḥesed (H2617, dispute_level 4) — the great covenant term: loving-kindness, steadfast love, covenant loyalty. Its meaning spans loyal love between treaty partners, parental compassion, and God's unfailing commitment to his covenant promises. The MKT renders it "steadfast love" throughout to preserve the covenantal force. hōdû (Hiphil imperative of yādāh, to give thanks, confess) — the imperative makes this an active call, not a passive observation. Structurally the psalm moves from creation (vv1–9) to Exodus/wilderness/conquest (vv10–22) to present remembrance and provision (vv23–25) before the closing doxology (v26).
2: hōdû lĕʾĕlōhê hāʾĕlōhîm kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "give thanks to the God above all gods — for his steadfast love endures forever" — ʾĕlōhê hāʾĕlōhîm (God of gods) — the superlative construction in Hebrew (noun + same noun in construct) expresses the highest degree: the God who stands supreme over every claim to divinity. This title appears also in Deut 10:17 and Dan 2:47 in similar contexts of absolute cosmic sovereignty.
3: hōdû lăʾdōnê hāʾădōnîm kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "give thanks to the Lord who reigns above all lords — for his steadfast love endures forever" — ʾădōnê hāʾădōnîm (Lord of lords) — the second superlative, using ʾādôn (lord, master, sovereign). The three opening verses form a triad: YHWH (personal covenant name, v1), God of gods (cosmic supremacy, v2), Lord of lords (sovereign mastery, v3). In Rev 17:14 and 19:16, "King of kings and Lord of lords" is applied directly to Christ, drawing directly on this language.
4: lĕʿōśēh niplāʾôt gĕdōlôt lĕbaddô kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "to him who alone performs great wonders — for his steadfast love endures forever" — niplāʾôt (wonders, marvelous deeds, from pālāʾ, to be extraordinary, surpassing) — the word group used for the Exodus miracles and the incomparable acts of God. lĕbaddô (alone, by himself) — no other agent shares credit for the great wonders. The grammar anticipates the creation hymn: what follows are the great wonders he alone has done.
5: lĕʿōśēh hašāmayim bitbûnāh kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "to him who made the heavens with wisdom — for his steadfast love endures forever" — bitbûnāh (with understanding, wisdom, discernment) — tĕbûnāh from bîn (to understand, discern); creation of the heavens is presented as an act of divine intelligence, echoing Prov 3:19 ("the LORD by wisdom founded the earth; by understanding he established the heavens").
6: lĕrōqaʿ hāʾāreṣ ʿal-hammayim kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "to him who laid the earth out over the waters — for his steadfast love endures forever" — rāqaʿ (to beat out, hammer flat, spread out) — used of hammering metal into sheets (Num 16:39); applied here to spreading the solid earth over the primordial waters (Gen 1:9–10; Job 37:18). The image is of the earth as a surface hammered out over the cosmic deep — cosmological imagery used as praise language, not scientific description.
7: lĕʿōśēh ʾôrîm gĕdōlîm kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "to him who made the great lights — for his steadfast love endures forever" — ʾôrîm gĕdōlîm (great lights) — deliberately generic terms matching Gen 1:16's usage. The avoidance of šemeš (sun) and yārēaḥ (moon), which were also names of celestial deities in surrounding cultures, is a polemical move: these are not divine beings but lights the LORD created and assigned their governance.
8: ʾet-haššemeš lĕmemšelet hayyôm kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "the sun to govern the day — for his steadfast love endures forever" — lĕmemšelet (for the rule/governance of) — from māšal (to rule, have dominion); the sun governs the day as a delegated authority, not as an independent divine power. Gen 1:16 uses the identical language: the great light is set to rule the day.
9: ʾet-hayyārēaḥ wĕkôkābîm lĕmemšĕlôt hallaylāh kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "the moon and stars to govern the night — for his steadfast love endures forever" — the moon shares governance with the stars — neither having independent authority. The singular rule over night contrasts with the single sun; the pairing of moon and stars may reflect their combined use in ancient calendrical reckoning.
10: lĕmakkēh miṣrayim bibkôrêhem kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "to him who struck down Egypt by killing their firstborn — for his steadfast love endures forever" — the historical credo begins. makkēh (participle of nākāh, to strike/smite) — the Exodus narrative's defining verb for the plagues. bibkôrêhem (in/among their firstborn) — the final plague is specified; the death of the firstborn broke Pharaoh's resistance and released Israel. The psalmist frames these Exodus judgments as expressions of ḥesed: the very judgment against Egypt is simultaneously covenant loyalty to Israel.
11: wayyôṣēʾ yiśrāʾēl mittôkām kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "who led Israel out from among them — for his steadfast love endures forever" — wayyôṣēʾ (Hiphil consecutive of yāṣāʾ, cause to go out) — the paradigmatic Exodus verb; the same word in the Decalogue: "I am the LORD your God who brought you out (yāṣāʾ) of Egypt" (Exod 20:2). The leading out "from among them" (mittôkām) implies extraction from the midst of the Egyptians — the Exodus as liberation from a surrounding hostile environment.
12: bĕyād ḥăzāqāh ûbizrōaʿ nĕṭûyāh kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "with a powerful hand and an arm stretched wide — for his steadfast love endures forever" — yād ḥăzāqāh (strong hand) and zĕrōaʿ nĕṭûyāh (outstretched arm) — the formulaic pair from the Exodus tradition (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:19; 26:8; Ezek 20:33–34). The pair occurs so frequently in the Deuteronomic credo that it functions as a summary for the entire redemptive act. The "stretched arm" imagery invokes the warrior stance — YHWH fighting for Israel as their divine warrior.
13: lĕgōzēr yam-sûp ligzārîm kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "to him who split the Reed/Red Sea apart — for his steadfast love endures forever" — gāzar (to cut, split, divide) — the verb used for the sea's division; both bāqaʿ and gāzar emphasize a violent cleaving apart of the waters. yam-sûp (Sea of Reeds) — the Hebrew is "sea of reeds" (sûp = reeds, papyrus), likely the papyrus marshes at the northern Suez region rather than the deep Red Sea proper, though the tradition was rendered "Red Sea" in the LXX.
14: wĕheʿěbîr yiśrāʾēl bĕtôkô kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "who led Israel safely through its midst — for his steadfast love endures forever" — heʿěbîr (Hiphil of ʿābar, to cause to pass through) — the same root as the Passover crossing; Israel is led through the divided sea, not around it. The path through the midst of the sea is the signature miraculous act that generates the entire hymnic tradition (Exod 15; Ps 66:6; Isa 51:10).
15: wĕniʿēr parʿōh wĕḥêlô bĕyam-sûp kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "and swept Pharaoh and his army into the Reed Sea — for his steadfast love endures forever" — niʿēr (Piel of nāʿar, to shake off, to sweep away) — a verb of violent shaking; Pharaoh and his forces are shaken into the sea like crumbs shaken from a cloth. Exod 14:27 uses nēʿar for the same event: the LORD "threw" the Egyptians into the midst of the sea.
16: lĕmôlîk ʿammô bammidbar kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "to him who guided his people through the desert — for his steadfast love endures forever" — mōlîk (Hiphil participle of hālak, cause to walk, lead) — the forty-year wilderness period — with its rebellion, murmuring, and divine provision — is summarized in a single participial phrase and presented as an act of covenant ḥesed: YHWH guiding Israel through uninhabitable terrain.
17: lĕmakkēh mĕlākîm gĕdōlîm kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "to him who defeated great kings — for his steadfast love endures forever" — the conquest summary begins with the same participle (makkēh, striking/smiting) used for the Exodus plagues (v10), creating a structural parallel: the same divine warrior who struck Egypt struck the Canaanite kings.
18: wayyahărōg mĕlākîm ʾaddîrîm kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "who slew renowned kings — for his steadfast love endures forever" — ʾaddîrîm (renowned, majestic, powerful, from ʾādar, to be great, glorious) — a stronger term than gĕdōlîm (great); these are kings of distinction and renown. The escalation (great → renowned) prepares for the named exemplars in vv19–20.
19: lĕSîḥôn melek hāʾĕmōrî kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "Sihon, king of the Amorites — for his steadfast love endures forever" — Sihon denied Israel passage and was defeated at Jahaz (Num 21:21–25; Deut 2:26–37). His defeat opened the Transjordanian campaign. In the biblical tradition Sihon and Og became paradigmatic examples of YHWH's conquest power, cited together in Num 21, Deut 1–4, Josh 2, Neh 9, and Pss 135–136.
20: ûlĕʿôg melek habbāšān kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "and Og, king of Bashan — for his steadfast love endures forever" — Og, remembered for his enormous iron bed (Deut 3:11), was the last of the Rephaim. His defeat at Edrei (Num 21:33–35) completed Israel's control of Transjordan. The brevity of his mention signals how established the tradition was: the audience needed only the name to recall the full episode.
21: wĕnātan ʾarṣām lĕnāḥălāh kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "who gave their land as an inheritance — for his steadfast love endures forever" — lĕnāḥălāh (as an inheritance/heritage) — the goal of the conquest is not military glory but the inheritance promised to Abraham (Gen 15:18–21; 17:8). The land is received as inheritance, carrying both the legal weight of patrimonial property and the theological weight of covenant fulfillment.
22: nāḥălāh lĕyiśrāʾēl ʿabdô kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "an inheritance given to Israel his servant — for his steadfast love endures forever" — the repetition of nāḥălāh from v21 is a resumptive doublet (resumptio) that makes the inheritance emphatic by restating it with added specificity. ʿabdô (his servant, singular) — Israel collectively is YHWH's servant, recalling the Servant Songs of Isaiah and the covenant-servant language of Deut 32:36.
23: šebekišpālēnû zākar lānû kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "who remembered us when we were brought low — for his steadfast love endures forever" — šebekišpālēnû (when we were brought low, humiliated) — from šāpēl (to be low, humble), here Niphal participle: the humbled, abased state. zākar lānû (he remembered us) — the divine remembering (zākar) is active intervention; when God "remembers" in the Hebrew Bible he acts on behalf of the remembered (Gen 8:1; Exod 2:24). The verse shifts from historical narrative to confessional first-person plural, making the psalm contemporary for any generation in distress.
24: wayyiprĕqēnû miṣṣārênû kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "and delivered us from our enemies — for his steadfast love endures forever" — yiprĕqēnû (Hiphil of pāraq, to tear away, rescue by force, deliver) — a verb with connotations of violent rupture: the enemies' grip is broken by force. ṣārênû (our adversaries, those who press us) — from ṣārar (to press, confine, be hostile).
25: nōtēn leḥem lĕkol-bāśār kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "who provides food for every living thing — for his steadfast love endures forever" — kol-bāśār (every flesh, every living creature) — the scope is universal, not restricted to Israel; the creational providence of v25 is broader than the covenant history of vv10–24. The psalm closes with creation before the final doxology (v26), forming a bracket with the creation section (vv4–9).
26: hōdû lĕʾēl haššāmayim kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô — "give thanks to the God of heaven — for his steadfast love endures forever" — ʾēl haššāmayim (God of heaven) — a title used in the post-exilic period and in Persian-era texts (Ezra 1:2; Neh 1:4; Dan 2:19) — which may indicate a late composition or adaptation. The closing call to thanks brackets the psalm with the opening hōdû (v1), and the 26th occurrence of kî lĕʿôlām ḥasdô closes the liturgical anthem. The number 26 matches the numerical value of the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) in gematria — whether intentional or coincidental, the structural completeness is striking.
